test.name: One death certificate, two informants, two information qualities
test.description: Assertions a_011 (death: 'Died 12 March 1908') and a_012 (birth: 'Born 1845, Pennsylvania') come from the SAME source, src_004, Patrick Flynn's 1908 death certificate (an original source). But they have different informants: the attending physician witnessed the death (primary), while the son-in-law James Brown reported the birth he was not present for six decades earlier (secondary). The skill must classify per-assertion, not per-source — a single original source legitimately carries both primary and secondary information.

input.user_message: Review the information-quality classification on a_011 and a_012 — both extracted from Patrick Flynn's 1908 death certificate — and make sure each reflects its own informant.

judge_context: Should classify a_011 (death date) as PRIMARY — the attending physician witnessed the death first-hand; proximity 'witness'
Should classify a_012 (birth fact) as SECONDARY — informant James Brown, the son-in-law, was not present at a birth ~63 years before; proximity 'family_not_present'

Should explicitly state that the same original source (the 1908 death certificate) carries BOTH primary and secondary information because the informant differs per fact — classification is per-assertion, never per-source, and Layer 1 (original) does not force Layer 2
